content.text:
This is a **legitimate security concern** — the reporter found that Docker sandbox containers can read host metadata from `/proc` and `/sys` (hostname, SSD model, LUKS UUIDs, etc.), even when those paths are in `tools.fs.deny_paths`. The `deny_paths` config only applies to Moltis's file-access tool, not to what the container kernel exposes.

Let me check how the sandbox currently mounts these filesystems.

input.prompt:
I need to understand how the Docker sandbox is configured, specifically whether /proc and /sys are mounted or restricted inside containers. 

Look at:
1. `crates/tools/src/sandbox.rs` — the sandbox trait and Docker implementation
2. Any Docker run command construction — look for how containers are started, what flags are passed (--security-opt, --cap-drop, volume mounts, etc.)
3. Whether /proc or /sys are mentioned anywhere in the codebase
4. Any seccomp profiles or AppArmor configs

Focus on finding the exact code that starts Docker containers and what security restrictions are applied. Report the relevant code sections with file paths and line numbers.

1→# Sandbox Backends
     2→
     3→Moltis runs LLM-generated commands inside containers to protect your host
     4→system. The sandbox backend controls which container technology is used.
     5→
     6→## Backend Selection
     7→
     8→Configure in `moltis.toml`:
     9→
    10→```toml
    11→[tools.exec.sandbox]
    12→backend = "auto"          # default — picks the best available
    13→# backend = "podman"      # force Podman (daemonless, rootless)
    14→# backend = "docker"      # force Docker
    15→# backend = "apple-container"  # force Apple Container (macOS only)
    16→# backend = "wasm"        # force WASM sandbox (Wasmtime + WASI)
    17→# backend = "restricted-host"  # env clearing + rlimits only
    18→```
    19→
    20→With `"auto"` (the default), Moltis picks the strongest available backend:
    21→
    22→| Priority | Backend           | Platform | Isolation          |
    23→|----------|-------------------|----------|--------------------|
    24→| 1        | Apple Container   | macOS    | VM (Virtualization.framework) |
    25→| 2        | Podman            | any      | Linux namespaces / cgroups (daemonless) |
    26→| 3        | Docker            | any      | Linux namespaces / cgroups    |
    27→| 4        | Restricted Host   | any      | env clearing, rlimits (no filesystem isolation) |
    28→| 5        | none (host)       | any      | no isolation                  |
    29→
    30→The WASM backend (`backend = "wasm"`) is not in the auto-detect chain because
    31→it cannot execute arbitrary shell commands — use it explicitly when you want
    32→WASI-isolated execution.
    33→
    34→## Apple Container (recommended on macOS)
    35→
    36→[Apple Container](https://github.com/apple/container) runs each sandbox in a
    37→lightweight virtual machine using Apple's Virtualization.framework. Every
    38→container gets its own kernel, so a kernel exploit inside the sandbox cannot
    39→reach the host — unlike Docker, which shares the host kernel.
    40→
    41→### Install
    42→
    43→Download the signed installer from GitHub:
    44→
    45→```bash
    46→# Download the installer package
    47→gh release download --repo apple/container --pattern "container-installer-signed.pkg" --dir /tmp
    48→
    49→# Install (requires admin)
    50→sudo installer -pkg /tmp/container-installer-signed.pkg -target /
    51→
    52→# First-time setup — downloads a default Linux kernel
    53→container system start
    54→```
    55→
    56→Alternatively, build from source with `brew install container` (requires
    57→Xcode 26+).
    58→
    59→### Verify
    60→
    61→```bash
    62→container --version
    63→# Run a quick test
    64→container run --rm ubuntu echo "hello from VM"
    65→```
    66→
    67→Once installed, restart `moltis gateway` — the startup banner will show
    68→`sandbox: apple-container backend`.
    69→
    70→## Podman
    71→
    72→[Podman](https://podman.io/) is a daemonless, rootless container engine that
    73→is CLI-compatible with Docker. It is preferred over Docker in auto-detection
    74→because it doesn't require a background daemon process and runs rootless by
    75→default for better security.
    76→
    77→### Install
    78→
    79→```bash
    80→# macOS
    81→brew install podman
    82→podman machine init && podman machine start
    83→
    84→# Debian/Ubuntu
    85→sudo apt-get install -y podman
    86→
    87→# Fedora/RHEL
    88→sudo dnf install -y podman
    89→```
    90→
    91→### Verify
    92→
    93→```bash
    94→podman --version
    95→podman run --rm docker.io/library/ubuntu echo "hello from podman"
    96→```
    97→
    98→Once installed, restart `moltis gateway` — the startup banner will show
    99→`sandbox: podman backend`. All Docker hardening flags (see below) apply
   100→identically to Podman containers.
   101→
   102→## Docker
   103→
   104→Docker is supported on macOS, Linux, and Windows. On macOS it runs inside a
   105→Linux VM managed by Docker Desktop, so it is reasonably isolated but adds more
   106→overhead than Apple Container.
   107→
   108→Install from https://docs.docker.com/get-docker/
   109→
   110→### Docker/Podman Hardening
   111→
   112→Docker and Podman containers launched by Moltis include the following security
   113→hardening flags by default:
   114→
   115→| Flag | Effect |
   116→|------|--------|
   117→| `--cap-drop ALL` | Drops all Linux capabilities |
   118→| `--security-opt no-new-privileges` | Prevents privilege escalation via setuid/setgid binaries |
   119→| `--tmpfs /tmp:rw,nosuid,size=256m` | Writable tmpfs for temp files (noexec on real root) |
   120→| `--tmpfs /run:rw,nosuid,size=64m` | Writable tmpfs for runtime files |
   121→| `--read-only` | Read-only root filesystem (prebuilt images only) |
   122→
   123→The `--read-only` flag is applied only to prebuilt sandbox images (where
   124→packages are already baked in). Non-prebuilt images need a writable root
   125→filesystem for `apt-get` provisioning on first start.
   126→
   127→## WASM Sandbox (Wasmtime + WASI)
   128→
   129→The WASM sandbox provides real sandboxed execution using
   130→[Wasmtime](https://wasmtime.dev/) with WASI. Commands execute in an isolated
   131→filesystem tree with fuel metering and epoch-based timeout enforcement.
   132→
   133→### How It Works
   134→
   135→The WASM sandbox has two execution tiers:
   136→
   137→**Tier 1 — Built-in commands** (~20 common coreutils implemented in Rust):
   138→`echo`, `cat`, `ls`, `mkdir`, `rm`, `cp`, `mv`, `pwd`, `env`, `head`, `tail`,
   139→`wc`, `sort`, `touch`, `which`, `true`, `false`, `test`/`[`, `basename`,
   140→`dirname`.
   141→
   142→These operate on a sandboxed directory tree, translating guest paths (e.g.
   143→`/home/sandbox/file.txt`) to host paths under `~/.moltis/sandbox/wasm/<id>/`.
   144→Paths outside the sandbox root are rejected.
   145→
   146→Basic shell features are supported: `&&`, `||`, `;` sequences, `$VAR`
   147→expansion, quoting via `shell-words`, and `>` / `>>` output redirects.
   148→
   149→**Tier 2 — Real WASM module execution**: When the command references a `.wasm`
   150→file, it is loaded and run via Wasmtime + WASI preview1 with full isolation:
   151→preopened directories, fuel metering, epoch interruption, and captured I/O.
   152→
   153→**Unknown commands** return exit code 127: "command not found in WASM sandbox".
   154→
   155→### Filesystem Isolation
   156→
   157→```
   158→~/.moltis/sandbox/wasm/<session-key>/
   159→  home/        preopened as /home/sandbox (rw)
   160→  tmp/         preopened as /tmp (rw)
   161→```
   162→
   163→Home persistence is respected:
   164→- `shared`: uses `data_dir()/sandbox/home/shared/wasm/`
   165→- `session`: uses `data_dir()/sandbox/wasm/<session-id>/`
   166→- `off`: per-session, cleaned up on `cleanup()`
   167→
   168→### Resource Limits
   169→
   170→- **Fuel metering**: `store.set_fuel(fuel_limit)` — limits WASM instruction
   171→  count (Tier 2 only)
   172→- **Epoch interruption**: background thread ticks epochs, store traps on
   173→  deadline (Tier 2 only)
   174→- **Memory**: `wasm_config.memory_reservation(bytes)` — Wasmtime memory limits
   175→  (Tier 2 only)
   176→
   177→### Configuration
   178→
   179→```toml
   180→[tools.exec.sandbox]
   181→backend = "wasm"
   182→
   183→# WASM-specific settings
   184→wasm_fuel_limit = 1000000000       # instruction fuel (default: 1 billion)
   185→wasm_epoch_interval_ms = 100       # epoch interruption interval (default: 100ms)
   186→
   187→[tools.exec.sandbox.resource_limits]
   188→memory_limit = "512M"    # Wasmtime memory reservation
   189→```
   190→
   191→### Limitations
   192→
   193→- Built-in commands cover common coreutils but not a full shell
   194→- No pipe support yet (planned via busybox.wasm in future)
   195→- No network access from WASM modules
   196→- `.wasm` modules must target WASI preview1
   197→
   198→### When to Use
   199→
   200→The WASM sandbox is a good fit when:
   201→
   202→- You want filesystem-isolated execution without container overhead
   203→- You need a sandboxed environment on platforms without Docker or Apple
   204→  Container
   205→- You are running `.wasm` modules and want fuel-metered, time-bounded execution
   206→
   207→### Compile-Time Feature
   208→
   209→The WASM sandbox is gated behind the `wasm` cargo feature, which is enabled by
   210→default. To build without Wasmtime (saves ~30 MB binary size):
   211→
   212→```bash
   213→cargo build --release --no-default-features --features lightweight
   214→```
   215→
   216→When the feature is disabled and the config requests `backend = "wasm"`, Moltis
   217→falls back to `restricted-host` with a warning.
   218→
   219→## Restricted Host Sandbox
   220→
   221→The restricted-host sandbox provides lightweight isolation by running commands
   222→on the host via `sh -c` with environment clearing and `ulimit` resource
   223→wrappers. This is the fallback when no container runtime is available.
   224→
   225→### How It Works
   226→
   227→When the restricted-host sandbox runs a command, it:
   228→
   229→1. **Clears the environment** — all inherited environment variables are removed
   230→2. **Sets a restricted PATH** — only `/usr/local/bin:/usr/bin:/bin`
   231→3. **Sets HOME to `/tmp`** — prevents access to the user's home directory
   232→4. **Applies resource limits** via shell `ulimit`:
   233→   - `ulimit -u` (max processes) from `pids_max` config (default: 256)
   234→   - `ulimit -n 1024` (max open files)
   235→   - `ulimit -t` (CPU seconds) from `cpu_quota` config (default: 300s)
   236→   - `ulimit -v` (virtual memory) from `memory_limit` config (default: 512M)
   237→5. **Enforces a timeout** via `tokio::time::timeout`
   238→
   239→User-specified environment variables from `opts.env` are re-applied after the
   240→environment is cleared, so the LLM tool can still pass required variables.
   241→
   242→### Limitations
   243→
   244→- No filesystem isolation — commands run on the host filesystem
   245→- No network isolation — commands can make network requests
   246→- `ulimit` enforcement is best-effort
   247→- No image building — `moltis sandbox build` returns immediately
   248→
   249→For production use with untrusted workloads, prefer Apple Container or Docker.
   250→
   251→## No sandbox
   252→
   253→If no runtime is found (and the `wasm` feature is disabled), commands execute
   254→directly on the host. The startup banner will show a warning. This is **not
   255→recommended** for untrusted workloads.
   256→
   257→## Failover Chain
   258→
   259→Moltis wraps the primary sandbox backend with automatic failover:
   260→
   261→- **Apple Container → Docker → Restricted Host**: if Apple Container enters a
   262→  corrupted state (stale metadata, missing config, VM boot failure), Moltis
   263→  fails over to Docker. If Docker is unavailable, it uses restricted-host.
   264→- **Docker → Restricted Host**: if Docker loses its daemon connection during a
   265→  session, Moltis fails over to the restricted-host sandbox.
   266→
   267→Failover is sticky for the lifetime of the gateway process — once triggered,
   268→all subsequent commands use the fallback backend. Restart the gateway to retry
   269→the primary backend.
   270→
   271→Failover triggers:
   272→
   273→| Primary | Triggers |
   274→|---------|----------|
   275→| Apple Container | `config.json missing`, `VM never booted`, `NSPOSIXErrorDomain Code=22`, service errors |
   276→| Docker | `cannot connect to the docker daemon`, `connection refused`, `is the docker daemon running` |
   277→
   278→## Per-session overrides
   279→
   280→The web UI allows toggling sandboxing per session and selecting a custom
   281→container image. These overrides persist across gateway restarts.
   282→
   283→## Home persistence
   284→
   285→By default, `/home/sandbox` is persisted in a shared host folder so that CLI
   286→auth/config files survive container recreation. You can change this with
   287→`home_persistence`:
   288→
   289→```toml
   290→[tools.exec.sandbox]
   291→home_persistence = "session"   # "off", "session", or "shared" (default)
   292→# shared_home_dir = "/path/to/shared-home"  # optional, used when mode is "shared"
   293→```
   294→
   295→- `off`: no home mount, container home is ephemeral
   296→- `session`: mount a per-session host folder to `/home/sandbox`
   297→- `shared`: mount one shared host folder to `/home/sandbox` for all sessions
   298→  (defaults to `data_dir()/sandbox/home/shared`, or `shared_home_dir` if set)
   299→
   300→Moltis stores persisted homes under `data_dir()/sandbox/home/`.
   301→
   302→## Docker-in-Docker workspace mounts
   303→
   304→When Moltis runs inside a container and launches Docker-backed sandboxes via a
   305→mounted container socket, the sandbox bind mount source must be a host-visible
   306→path. Moltis auto-detects this by inspecting the parent container's mounts. If
   307→that lookup fails or you want to pin the value explicitly, set
   308→`host_data_dir`:
   309→
   310→```toml
   311→[tools.exec.sandbox]
   312→host_data_dir = "/srv/moltis/data"
   313→```
   314→
   315→This remaps sandbox workspace mounts and default sandbox persistence paths from
   316→the guest `data_dir()` to the host path you provide. It is mainly an override
   317→for Docker-in-Docker deployments where mount auto-detection is unavailable or
   318→ambiguous.
   319→
   320→## Network policy
   321→
   322→By default, sandbox containers have no network access (`no_network = true`).
   323→For tasks that need filtered internet access, use
   324→[trusted network mode](trusted-network.md) — a proxy-based allowlist that
   325→lets containers reach approved domains while blocking everything else.
   326→
   327→```toml
   328→[tools.exec.sandbox]
   329→network = "trusted"
   330→trusted_domains = ["registry.npmjs.org", "github.com"]
   331→```
   332→
   333→See [Trusted Network](trusted-network.md) for full configuration and the
   334→network audit log.
   335→
   336→> **Note**: Home persistence applies to Docker, Apple Container, and WASM
   337→> backends. The restricted-host backend uses `HOME=/tmp` and does not mount
   338→> persistent storage.
   339→
   340→## Resource limits
   341→
   342→```toml
   343→[tools.exec.sandbox.resource_limits]
   344→memory_limit = "512M"
   345→cpu_quota = 1.0
   346→pids_max = 256
   347→```
   348→
   349→How resource limits are applied depends on the backend:
   350→
   351→| Limit | Docker | Apple Container | WASM | Restricted Host | cgroup (Linux) |
   352→|-------|--------|-----------------|------|-----------------|----------------|
   353→| `memory_limit` | `--memory` | `--memory` | Wasmtime reservation | `ulimit -v` | `MemoryMax=` |

   355→| `pids_max` | `--pids-limit` | `--pids-limit` | n/a | `ulimit -u` | `TasksMax=` |
   356→
   357→## Comparison
   358→
   359→| Feature | Apple Container | Docker | WASM | Restricted Host | none |
   360→|---------|----------------|--------|------|-----------------|------|
   361→| Filesystem isolation | ✅ VM boundary | ✅ namespaces | ✅ sandboxed tree | ❌ host FS | ❌ |

   366→| Image building | ✅ (via Docker) | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
   367→| Shell commands | ✅ full shell | ✅ full shell | ~20 built-ins | ✅ full shell | ✅ full shell |
   368→| Platform | macOS 26+ | any | any | any | any |
   369→| Overhead | low | medium | minimal | minimal | none |

## Docker Sandbox Configuration - Security Analysis

Based on my search of the Moltis codebase, here's what I found regarding Docker sandbox configuration, specifically regarding `/proc` and `/sys` mounting and security restrictions:

### Key Findings

**1. /proc and /sys mounting:**
- **No explicit restrictions on /proc or /sys**: The Docker sandbox implementation does NOT explicitly mount, restrict, or block `/proc` and `/sys` inside containers
- **Default Docker behavior**: `/proc` and `/sys` are mounted by default in Docker containers unless explicitly restricted
- **The only reference to /proc in the sandbox code** is in `/crates/tools/src/sandbox/paths.rs:86` where it reads `/proc/self/cgroup` from the **host** to detect if Moltis itself is running in a container (not sandbox-side code)

### Security Hardening Flags Applied

**File**: `/Users/penso/.superset/worktrees/moltis/tabby-poppyseed/crates/tools/src/sandbox/docker.rs:182-197`

The `hardening_args()` function applies these security flags to all Docker containers:

```rust
pub(crate) fn hardening_args(is_prebuilt: bool) -> Vec<String> {
    let mut args = vec![
        "--cap-drop".to_string(),
        "ALL".to_string(),
        "--security-opt".to_string(),
        "no-new-privileges".to_string(),
        "--tmpfs".to_string(),
        "/tmp:rw,nosuid,size=256m".to_string(),
        "--tmpfs".to_string(),
        "/run:rw,nosuid,size=64m".to_string(),
    ];
    if is_prebuilt {
        args.push("--read-only".to_string());
    }
    args
}
```

**Security settings:**
- `--cap-drop ALL` — drops all Linux capabilities (lines 184-185)
- `--security-opt no-new-privileges` — prevents privilege escalation via setuid/setgid (lines 186-187)
- `--tmpfs /tmp:rw,nosuid,size=256m` — writable tmpfs without execute permission on suid (lines 188-189)
- `--tmpfs /run:rw,nosuid,size=64m` — writable tmpfs for runtime files (lines 190-191)
- `--read-only` — read-only root filesystem for prebuilt images only (lines 193-195)

### Volume Mounts

**File**: `/Users/penso/.superset/worktrees/moltis/tabby-poppyseed/crates/tools/src/sandbox/docker.rs:199-225`

The sandbox mounts:
- **Workspace**: Data directory (read-only or read-write depending on config)
- **Home persistence**: `/home/sandbox` mounted from host

**Network isolation:**
- Default: `--network=none` (blocked)
- Trusted network mode: uses `--add-host=host.docker.internal:<gateway>` for filtered outbound access

### What's NOT restricted

- **`/proc` and `/sys`** are accessible in containers (default Docker behavior)
- **Kernel access** is shared (unlike Apple Container which uses separate VMs)
- No seccomp profiles explicitly defined in the code
- No AppArmor profiles defined in the code

### Related Tests

**File**: `/Users/penso/.superset/worktrees/moltis/tabby-poppyseed/crates/tools/src/sandbox/tests/core.rs:19-42`

Tests verify that hardening args are applied:
- Lines 19-28: verify prebuilt image hardening includes `--cap-drop`, `--security-opt`, `--read-only`, and tmpfs mounts
- Lines 32-42: verify non-prebuilt images have hardening but NOT `--read-only` (for `apt-get` provisioning)

### Documentation

**File**: `/Users/penso/.superset/worktrees/moltis/tabby-poppyseed/docs/src/sandbox.md:110-125`

Documents that Docker/Podman hardening includes the same flags shown above. No mention of `/proc`/`/sys` restrictions.

### Summary

The Docker/Podman sandbox in Moltis applies strong capability dropping and privilege escalation prevention, but **does not explicitly restrict access to `/proc` and `/sys`**. These pseudo-filesystems are accessible by default as they are in standard Docker containers. If you need to restrict `/proc` and `/sys`, you would need to add additional `--security-opt` flags or use a more restrictive sandbox backend like Apple Container (macOS only, uses separate VMs) or WASM (no network/shell, heavily restricted).

**Yes, this is a real security issue** — moderate severity, information disclosure class.

**The problem:** The Docker sandbox applies good hardening (`--cap-drop ALL`, `--security-opt no-new-privileges`, read-only root), but `/proc` and `/sys` are mounted with default Docker behavior. This leaks host metadata:

- `/proc` — hostname, kernel version, CPU info, memory layout, cgroup paths
- `/sys` — disk models, LUKS UUIDs, hardware serial numbers, device topology

The `deny_paths` config only controls Moltis's file-access tool — it doesn't restrict what the LLM can read via shell commands (`cat /proc/hostname`) inside the container.

**The fix** would be in `crates/tools/src/sandbox/docker.rs:182` — the `hardening_args()` function — by adding:

1. **`--tmpfs /proc:ro,nosuid`** or a custom seccomp/AppArmor profile masking sensitive proc entries
2. Docker's built-in `--pid=host` is already NOT used (good), but `procfs` still exposes host info when running with default Docker
3. Better: add `--security-opt=seccomp=<profile>` with a restrictive seccomp profile, or use Docker's `--cgroupns=private` plus masking:
   ```
   --tmpfs /proc/acpi:ro
   --read-only-paths /sys
   ```

Note: Apple Container (the macOS sandbox backend) is **not affected** since it uses a full VM with its own kernel — `/proc` and `/sys` reflect the VM, not the host.

identically to Podman containers.

## Docker

Docker is supported on macOS, Linux, and Windows. On macOS it runs inside a
Linux VM managed by Docker Desktop, so it is reasonably isolated but adds more
overhead than Apple Container.

Install from https://docs.docker.com/get-docker/

### Docker/Podman Hardening

Docker and Podman containers launched by Moltis include the following security
hardening flags by default:

| Flag | Effect |
|------|--------|
| `--cap-drop ALL` | Drops all Linux capabilities |
| `--security-opt no-new-privileges` | Prevents privilege escalation via setuid/setgid binaries |
| `--tmpfs /tmp:rw,nosuid,size=256m` | Writable tmpfs for temp files (noexec on real root) |
| `--tmpfs /run:rw,nosuid,size=64m` | Writable tmpfs for runtime files |
| `--read-only` | Read-only root filesystem (prebuilt images only) |

The `--read-only` flag is applied only to prebuilt sandbox images (where
packages are already baked in). Non-prebuilt images need a writable root
filesystem for `apt-get` provisioning on first start.

## WASM Sandbox (Wasmtime + WASI)

The WASM sandbox provides real sandboxed execution using
[Wasmtime](https://wasmtime.dev/) with WASI. Commands execute in an isolated
filesystem tree with fuel metering and epoch-based timeout enforcement.

### How It Works

The WASM sandbox has two execution tiers:

**Tier 1 — Built-in commands** (~20 common coreutils implemented in Rust):
`echo`, `cat`, `ls`, `mkdir`, `rm`, `cp`, `mv`, `pwd`, `env`, `head`, `tail`,
`wc`, `sort`, `touch`, `which`, `true`, `false`, `test`/`[`, `basename`,
`dirname`.

These operate on a sandboxed directory tree, translating guest paths (e.g.
`/home/sandbox/file.txt`) to host paths under `~/.moltis/sandbox/wasm/<id>/`.
Paths outside the sandbox root are rejected.

Basic shell features are supported: `&&`, `||`, `;` sequences, `$VAR`
expansion, quoting via `shell-words`, and `>` / `>>` output redirects.

**Tier 2 — Real WASM module execution**: When the command references a `.wasm`

# Sandbox Backends

Moltis runs LLM-generated commands inside containers to protect your host
system. The sandbox backend controls which container technology is used.

## Backend Selection

Configure in `moltis.toml`:

```toml
[tools.exec.sandbox]
backend = "auto"          # default — picks the best available
# backend = "podman"      # force Podman (daemonless, rootless)
# backend = "docker"      # force Docker
# backend = "apple-container"  # force Apple Container (macOS only)
# backend = "wasm"        # force WASM sandbox (Wasmtime + WASI)
# backend = "restricted-host"  # env clearing + rlimits only
```

With `"auto"` (the default), Moltis picks the strongest available backend:

| Priority | Backend           | Platform | Isolation          |
|----------|-------------------|----------|--------------------|
| 1        | Apple Container   | macOS    | VM (Virtualization.framework) |
| 2        | Podman            | any      | Linux namespaces / cgroups (daemonless) |
| 3        | Docker            | any      | Linux namespaces / cgroups    |
| 4        | Restricted Host   | any      | env clearing, rlimits (no filesystem isolation) |
| 5        | none (host)       | any      | no isolation                  |

The WASM backend (`backend = "wasm"`) is not in the auto-detect chain because
it cannot execute arbitrary shell commands — use it explicitly when you want
WASI-isolated execution.

## Apple Container (recommended on macOS)

[Apple Container](https://github.com/apple/container) runs each sandbox in a
lightweight virtual machine using Apple's Virtualization.framework. Every
container gets its own kernel, so a kernel exploit inside the sandbox cannot
reach the host — unlike Docker, which shares the host kernel.

### Install

Download the signed installer from GitHub:

```bash
# Download the installer package
gh release download --repo apple/container --pattern "container-installer-signed.pkg" --dir /tmp

# Install (requires admin)
sudo installer -pkg /tmp/container-installer-signed.pkg -target /

# First-time setup — downloads a default Linux kernel
container system start
```

Alternatively, build from source with `brew install container` (requires
Xcode 26+).

### Verify

```bash
container --version
# Run a quick test
container run --rm ubuntu echo "hello from VM"
```

Once installed, restart `moltis gateway` — the startup banner will show
`sandbox: apple-container backend`.

## Podman

[Podman](https://podman.io/) is a daemonless, rootless container engine that
is CLI-compatible with Docker. It is preferred over Docker in auto-detection
because it doesn't require a background daemon process and runs rootless by
default for better security.

### Install

```bash
# macOS
brew install podman
podman machine init && podman machine start

# Debian/Ubuntu
sudo apt-get install -y podman

# Fedora/RHEL
sudo dnf install -y podman
```

### Verify

```bash
podman --version
podman run --rm docker.io/library/ubuntu echo "hello from podman"
```

Once installed, restart `moltis gateway` — the startup banner will show
`sandbox: podman backend`. All Docker hardening flags (see below) apply
identically to Podman containers.

## Docker

Docker is supported on macOS, Linux, and Windows. On macOS it runs inside a
Linux VM managed by Docker Desktop, so it is reasonably isolated but adds more
overhead than Apple Container.

Install from https://docs.docker.com/get-docker/

### Docker/Podman Hardening

Docker and Podman containers launched by Moltis include the following security
hardening flags by default:

| Flag | Effect |
|------|--------|
| `--cap-drop ALL` | Drops all Linux capabilities |
| `--security-opt no-new-privileges` | Prevents privilege escalation via setuid/setgid binaries |
| `--tmpfs /tmp:rw,nosuid,size=256m` | Writable tmpfs for temp files (noexec on real root) |
| `--tmpfs /run:rw,nosuid,size=64m` | Writable tmpfs for runtime files |
| `--read-only` | Read-only root filesystem (prebuilt images only) |

The `--read-only` flag is applied only to prebuilt sandbox images (where
packages are already baked in). Non-prebuilt images need a writable root
filesystem for `apt-get` provisioning on first start.

## WASM Sandbox (Wasmtime + WASI)

The WASM sandbox provides real sandboxed execution using
[Wasmtime](https://wasmtime.dev/) with WASI. Commands execute in an isolated
filesystem tree with fuel metering and epoch-based timeout enforcement.

### How It Works

The WASM sandbox has two execution tiers:

**Tier 1 — Built-in commands** (~20 common coreutils implemented in Rust):
`echo`, `cat`, `ls`, `mkdir`, `rm`, `cp`, `mv`, `pwd`, `env`, `head`, `tail`,
`wc`, `sort`, `touch`, `which`, `true`, `false`, `test`/`[`, `basename`,
`dirname`.

These operate on a sandboxed directory tree, translating guest paths (e.g.
`/home/sandbox/file.txt`) to host paths under `~/.moltis/sandbox/wasm/<id>/`.
Paths outside the sandbox root are rejected.

Basic shell features are supported: `&&`, `||`, `;` sequences, `$VAR`
expansion, quoting via `shell-words`, and `>` / `>>` output redirects.

**Tier 2 — Real WASM module execution**: When the command references a `.wasm`
file, it is loaded and run via Wasmtime + WASI preview1 with full isolation:
preopened directories, fuel metering, epoch interruption, and captured I/O.

**Unknown commands** return exit code 127: "command not found in WASM sandbox".

### Filesystem Isolation

```
~/.moltis/sandbox/wasm/<session-key>/
  home/        preopened as /home/sandbox (rw)
  tmp/         preopened as /tmp (rw)
```

Home persistence is respected:
- `shared`: uses `data_dir()/sandbox/home/shared/wasm/`
- `session`: uses `data_dir()/sandbox/wasm/<session-id>/`
- `off`: per-session, cleaned up on `cleanup()`

### Resource Limits

- **Fuel metering**: `store.set_fuel(fuel_limit)` — limits WASM instruction
  count (Tier 2 only)
- **Epoch interruption**: background thread ticks epochs, store traps on
  deadline (Tier 2 only)
- **Memory**: `wasm_config.memory_reservation(bytes)` — Wasmtime memory limits
  (Tier 2 only)

### Configuration

```toml
[tools.exec.sandbox]
backend = "wasm"

# WASM-specific settings
wasm_fuel_limit = 1000000000       # instruction fuel (default: 1 billion)
wasm_epoch_interval_ms = 100       # epoch interruption interval (default: 100ms)

[tools.exec.sandbox.resource_limits]
memory_limit = "512M"    # Wasmtime memory reservation
```

### Limitations

- Built-in commands cover common coreutils but not a full shell
- No pipe support yet (planned via busybox.wasm in future)
- No network access from WASM modules
- `.wasm` modules must target WASI preview1

### When to Use

The WASM sandbox is a good fit when:

- You want filesystem-isolated execution without container overhead
- You need a sandboxed environment on platforms without Docker or Apple
  Container
- You are running `.wasm` modules and want fuel-metered, time-bounded execution

### Compile-Time Feature

The WASM sandbox is gated behind the `wasm` cargo feature, which is enabled by
default. To build without Wasmtime (saves ~30 MB binary size):

```bash
cargo build --release --no-default-features --features lightweight
```

When the feature is disabled and the config requests `backend = "wasm"`, Moltis
falls back to `restricted-host` with a warning.

## Restricted Host Sandbox

The restricted-host sandbox provides lightweight isolation by running commands
on the host via `sh -c` with environment clearing and `ulimit` resource
wrappers. This is the fallback when no container runtime is available.

### How It Works

When the restricted-host sandbox runs a command, it:

1. **Clears the environment** — all inherited environment variables are removed
2. **Sets a restricted PATH** — only `/usr/local/bin:/usr/bin:/bin`
3. **Sets HOME to `/tmp`** — prevents access to the user's home directory
4. **Applies resource limits** via shell `ulimit`:
   - `ulimit -u` (max processes) from `pids_max` config (default: 256)
   - `ulimit -n 1024` (max open files)
   - `ulimit -t` (CPU seconds) from `cpu_quota` config (default: 300s)
   - `ulimit -v` (virtual memory) from `memory_limit` config (default: 512M)
5. **Enforces a timeout** via `tokio::time::timeout`

User-specified environment variables from `opts.env` are re-applied after the
environment is cleared, so the LLM tool can still pass required variables.

### Limitations

- No filesystem isolation — commands run on the host filesystem
- No network isolation — commands can make network requests
- `ulimit` enforcement is best-effort
- No image building — `moltis sandbox build` returns immediately

For production use with untrusted workloads, prefer Apple Container or Docker.

## No sandbox

If no runtime is found (and the `wasm` feature is disabled), commands execute
directly on the host. The startup banner will show a warning. This is **not
recommended** for untrusted workloads.

## Failover Chain

Moltis wraps the primary sandbox backend with automatic failover:

- **Apple Container → Docker → Restricted Host**: if Apple Container enters a
  corrupted state (stale metadata, missing config, VM boot failure), Moltis
  fails over to Docker. If Docker is unavailable, it uses restricted-host.
- **Docker → Restricted Host**: if Docker loses its daemon connection during a
  session, Moltis fails over to the restricted-host sandbox.

Failover is sticky for the lifetime of the gateway process — once triggered,
all subsequent commands use the fallback backend. Restart the gateway to retry
the primary backend.

Failover triggers:

| Primary | Triggers |
|---------|----------|
| Apple Container | `config.json missing`, `VM never booted`, `NSPOSIXErrorDomain Code=22`, service errors |
| Docker | `cannot connect to the docker daemon`, `connection refused`, `is the docker daemon running` |

## Per-session overrides

The web UI allows toggling sandboxing per session and selecting a custom
container image. These overrides persist across gateway restarts.

## Home persistence

By default, `/home/sandbox` is persisted in a shared host folder so that CLI
auth/config files survive container recreation. You can change this with
`home_persistence`:

```toml
[tools.exec.sandbox]
home_persistence = "session"   # "off", "session", or "shared" (default)
# shared_home_dir = "/path/to/shared-home"  # optional, used when mode is "shared"
```

- `off`: no home mount, container home is ephemeral
- `session`: mount a per-session host folder to `/home/sandbox`
- `shared`: mount one shared host folder to `/home/sandbox` for all sessions
  (defaults to `data_dir()/sandbox/home/shared`, or `shared_home_dir` if set)

Moltis stores persisted homes under `data_dir()/sandbox/home/`.

## Docker-in-Docker workspace mounts

When Moltis runs inside a container and launches Docker-backed sandboxes via a
mounted container socket, the sandbox bind mount source must be a host-visible
path. Moltis auto-detects this by inspecting the parent container's mounts. If
that lookup fails or you want to pin the value explicitly, set
`host_data_dir`:

```toml
[tools.exec.sandbox]
host_data_dir = "/srv/moltis/data"
```

This remaps sandbox workspace mounts and default sandbox persistence paths from
the guest `data_dir()` to the host path you provide. It is mainly an override
for Docker-in-Docker deployments where mount auto-detection is unavailable or
ambiguous.

## Network policy

By default, sandbox containers have no network access (`no_network = true`).
For tasks that need filtered internet access, use
[trusted network mode](trusted-network.md) — a proxy-based allowlist that
lets containers reach approved domains while blocking everything else.

```toml
[tools.exec.sandbox]
network = "trusted"
trusted_domains = ["registry.npmjs.org", "github.com"]
```

See [Trusted Network](trusted-network.md) for full configuration and the
network audit log.

> **Note**: Home persistence applies to Docker, Apple Container, and WASM
> backends. The restricted-host backend uses `HOME=/tmp` and does not mount
> persistent storage.

## Resource limits

```toml
[tools.exec.sandbox.resource_limits]
memory_limit = "512M"
cpu_quota = 1.0
pids_max = 256
```

How resource limits are applied depends on the backend:

| Limit | Docker | Apple Container | WASM | Restricted Host | cgroup (Linux) |
|-------|--------|-----------------|------|-----------------|----------------|
| `memory_limit` | `--memory` | `--memory` | Wasmtime reservation | `ulimit -v` | `MemoryMax=` |
| `cpu_quota` | `--cpus` | `--cpus` | epoch timeout | `ulimit -t` (seconds) | `CPUQuota=` |
| `pids_max` | `--pids-limit` | `--pids-limit` | n/a | `ulimit -u` | `TasksMax=` |

## Comparison

| Feature | Apple Container | Docker | WASM | Restricted Host | none |
|---------|----------------|--------|------|-----------------|------|
| Filesystem isolation | ✅ VM boundary | ✅ namespaces | ✅ sandboxed tree | ❌ host FS | ❌ |
| Network isolation | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ (no network) | ❌ | ❌ |
| Kernel isolation | ✅ separate kernel | ❌ shared kernel | ✅ WASM VM | ❌ | ❌ |
| Environment isolation | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ cleared + restricted | ❌ |
| Resource limits | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ fuel + epoch | ✅ ulimit | ❌ |
| Image building | ✅ (via Docker) | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| Shell commands | ✅ full shell | ✅ full shell | ~20 built-ins | ✅ full shell | ✅ full shell |
| Platform | macOS 26+ | any | any | any | any |
| Overhead | low | medium | minimal | minimal | none |

lines: | `--tmpfs /tmp:rw,nosuid,size=256m` | Writable tmpfs for temp files (noexec on real root) |
+| `--tmpfs /sys/class/dmi:ro,nosuid` | Masks system serial numbers and identifiers |
+| `--tmpfs /sys/class/block:ro,nosuid` | Masks block device info (disk models, LUKS UUIDs) |
The `--read-only` flag is applied only to prebuilt sandbox images (where
packages are already baked in). Non-prebuilt images need a writable root
filesystem for `apt-get` provisioning on first start.
+The `/sys` tmpfs overlays prevent host hardware metadata (serial numbers, disk
+models, LUKS UUIDs) from being visible inside the container. Note that
+`tools.fs.deny_paths` only restricts Moltis file-access tools — these kernel
+filesystem masks prevent leakage via shell commands as well.

content.text:
Done. Here's what was changed:

**`crates/tools/src/sandbox/docker.rs`** — `hardening_args()` now adds:
- `--hostname sandbox` — container gets its own hostname instead of leaking the host's
- `--tmpfs /sys/firmware:ro,nosuid` — masks BIOS/UEFI data
- `--tmpfs /sys/class/dmi:ro,nosuid` — masks system serial numbers/identifiers
- `--tmpfs /sys/devices/virtual/dmi:ro,nosuid` — masks DMI attributes
- `--tmpfs /sys/class/block:ro,nosuid` — masks block device info (disk models, LUKS UUIDs)

All flags work identically on **Docker and Podman** (shared implementation via `DockerSandbox`). Apple Container is unaffected — it uses a separate VM with its own kernel.

**`crates/tools/src/sandbox/tests/core.rs`** — updated both hardening tests to assert the new flags.

**`docs/src/sandbox.md`** — documented the new hardening flags in the table and added an explanation paragraph.

diff --git a/crates/tools/src/sandbox/docker.rs b/crates/tools/src/sandbox/docker.rs
index bdb0cd9a..ac9e84a0 100644
--- a/crates/tools/src/sandbox/docker.rs
+++ b/crates/tools/src/sandbox/docker.rs
@@ -181,14 +181,33 @@ impl DockerSandbox {
     /// non-prebuilt images need a writable root for `apt-get` provisioning.
     pub(crate) fn hardening_args(is_prebuilt: bool) -> Vec<String> {
         let mut args = vec![
+            // --- Capability / privilege ---
             "--cap-drop".to_string(),
             "ALL".to_string(),
             "--security-opt".to_string(),
             "no-new-privileges".to_string(),
+            // --- Writable tmpfs mounts ---
             "--tmpfs".to_string(),
             "/tmp:rw,nosuid,size=256m".to_string(),
             "--tmpfs".to_string(),
             "/run:rw,nosuid,size=64m".to_string(),
+            // --- Host metadata isolation ---
+            // Give the container its own hostname so /proc/sys/kernel/hostname
+            // and the `hostname` command do not reveal the host identity.
+            "--hostname".to_string(),
+            "sandbox".to_string(),
+            // Mask /sys subtrees that expose host hardware identifiers
+            // (serial numbers, BIOS/UEFI data, disk models, LUKS UUIDs).
+            // Empty read-only tmpfs overlays hide the underlying sysfs entries
+            // and work identically on Docker and Podman.
+            "--tmpfs".to_string(),
+            "/sys/firmware:ro,nosuid".to_string(),
+            "--tmpfs".to_string(),
+            "/sys/class/dmi:ro,nosuid".to_string(),
+            "--tmpfs".to_string(),
+            "/sys/devices/virtual/dmi:ro,nosuid".to_string(),
+            "--tmpfs".to_string(),
+            "/sys/class/block:ro,nosuid".to_string(),
         ];
         if is_prebuilt {
             args.push("--read-only".to_string());

 | `--tmpfs /tmp:rw,nosuid,size=256m` | Writable tmpfs for temp files (noexec on real root) |
 | `--tmpfs /run:rw,nosuid,size=64m` | Writable tmpfs for runtime files |
 | `--read-only` | Read-only root filesystem (prebuilt images only) |
+| `--hostname sandbox` | Prevents host hostname leakage |
+| `--tmpfs /sys/firmware:ro,nosuid` | Masks BIOS/UEFI firmware data |
+| `--tmpfs /sys/class/dmi:ro,nosuid` | Masks system serial numbers and identifiers |
+| `--tmpfs /sys/devices/virtual/dmi:ro,nosuid` | Masks DMI attributes |
+| `--tmpfs /sys/class/block:ro,nosuid` | Masks block device info (disk models, LUKS UUIDs) |
 
 The `--read-only` flag is applied only to prebuilt sandbox images (where
 packages are already baked in). Non-prebuilt images need a writable root
 filesystem for `apt-get` provisioning on first start.
 
+The `/sys` tmpfs overlays prevent host hardware metadata (serial numbers, disk
+models, LUKS UUIDs) from being visible inside the container. Note that
+`tools.fs.deny_paths` only restricts Moltis file-access tools — these kernel
+filesystem masks prevent leakage via shell commands as well.
